protocol_definition.name: Manager-Worker
protocol_definition.message_flow: Manager assigns work, workers report, Manager resolves conflicts, Writer finalizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Manager sees all messages; workers see the task, assignment, and relevant summaries.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after Manager synthesis and Writer output or budget exhaustion.

final_call.expected_action: Produce the self-contained final deliverable after independently checking the Manager decision against every task constraint and verified worker result.

intermediate_messages.expected_action: Decompose the task and assign distinct subtasks to Planner, Researcher, Analyst, Critic, and Writer.
Complete the Planner assignment. Report evidence, assumptions, and unresolved issues to Manager.
Complete the Researcher assignment. Report evidence, assumptions, and unresolved issues to Manager.
Complete the Analyst assignment. Report evidence, assumptions, and unresolved issues to Manager.
Complete the Critic assignment. Report evidence, assumptions, and unresolved issues to Manager.
Complete the Writer assignment. Report evidence, assumptions, and unresolved issues to Manager.
intermediate_messages.content:
Executive Summary & The Video Production Dilemma
- Our mid-sized video production agency routinely handles 8K RAW footage where single files commonly exceed 100–150 GB. The team requires a cloud storage and collaboration platform that supports true block-level (delta) sync to minimize re-uploaded data, especially when editors work on incremental changes or collaborate across time zones.
- External collaborators (freelancers) must be able to upload large RAW assets directly through shared links without consuming paid seats, while preserving security controls and avoiding friction in the review/approval cycle.
- The annual licensing budget is capped at $25,000 for 75 internal users. Any platform choice must align with this maximum while delivering enterprise-grade security, granular access controls, and reliable performance for large-file workflows.
- The decision must balance performance (delta/block-level sync), external contributor ease, security (expiring links, password protection, granular permissions), and total cost of ownership.

Comprehensive Comparison Matrix
Note: The figures below are indicative based on typical enterprise offerings and public documentation. Exact pricing and feature specifics should be confirmed with the vendors for a formal proposal.

| Platform & Tier | Estimated Annual Cost (75 Users) | Max Single File Upload Limit | Block-Level (Delta) Sync Support | External Free Upload Capabilities |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dropbox Advanced Plan | Estimated: $15,000–$20,000/year (depending on region and contract) | Individual file uploads commonly supported up to large sizes; recent enterprise notes indicate large file transfers but verify exact per-file cap | Block-level (delta) sync capability is not widely documented for 8K+ RAW in the Advanced tier; performance for large 100+ GB files has historically varied by network; require confirmation of true delta sync for very large binaries | External collaborators can upload via shared links; however, many enterprise SKUs require collaborators to have at least a basic account or paid seats for advanced collaboration; confirm if guest/anonymous uploads are allowed and any size/scoping limits |
| Box Enterprise Plan | Estimated: $18,000–$25,000/year (tiered by user count and add-ons) | Box supports large files; enterprise demos show large-file sharing within shared links | Box supports robust synchronization via Box Drive and cloud sync; delta/diff sync specifics for 8K RAW on enterprise tiers are not always publicly stated; needs verification for true block-level delta capabilities at file sizes >100 GB | Box enables secure file sharing via links with expiration and password protections; external collaborators can upload via shared links if enabled, but often requires a Box account or guest access; confirm constraints on unauthenticated guest uploads and size limits |
| Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2 | Estimated: within $25,000/year (typical per-user licensing around $10–$15/mo; exact pricing depends on volume and agreements) | OneDrive handles large files; SharePoint/OneDrive support for large file sizes has improved; precise per-file cap for enterprise may require confirmation for 100–150 GB RAW files | Block-level delta sync (differences) in OneDrive/SharePoint ecosystem is limited; Microsoft has historically optimized for cloud sync rather than granular delta syncing for ultra-large binary files; confirm current capabilities with engineering advisories | External collaborators can upload via secure links; guest access and anonymous uploads may be restricted; often requires recipients to have a Microsoft account or guest access with permissions; verify if anonymous large-file uploads are allowed without paid seats and any security controls |

Notes on the matrix:
- Exact annual costs depend on negotiated enterprise terms, seat counts, add-ons (Box or Dropbox add-ons for governance, security), and regional pricing. The range estimates above are for planning purposes and should be validated with RFPs.
- Max single-file upload limits are typically governed by vendor defaults and practical performance constraints. For 8K RAW in the 100–150 GB range, you must verify with each vendor’s documented limits or discuss accommodations (e.g., chunked uploads, multi-part upload, and any recommended client tooling).
- Block-level (delta) sync is critical for our use case. Publicly stated delta-sync capabilities for ultra-large binaries are less consistently documented; this requires direct vendor validation with engineering or a proof-of-concept to confirm true delta synchronization for multi-hundred-GB RAW assets.
- External free upload capabilities matter for freelancers. Some platforms impose login requirements or guest/anonymous upload limits; others require a paid seat for full feature parity or impose file-size caps on shared links. A practical path is to confirm whether anonymous or guest-link uploads are allowed for 100+ GB files and what security controls exist (expiration, password, ACLs).

Trend Identification
- The industry is shifting from on-prem NAS to Hybrid Cloud Video Workflows. Teams increasingly rely on cloud-enabled collaboration, edge caching, and selective streaming/approval. Cloud platforms enable distributed editing, centralized governance, and rapid sharing with external collaborators, while preserving the ability to scale storage up or down and reduce local hardware debt.

Final Strategic Recommendation
- Given the requirements — true block-level delta sync for 8K RAW files, external collaborator uploads via links without paid seats, strict cost ceiling, and robust link-security controls — Box Enterprise emerges as the most compelling platform among those evaluated, provided that:
  - Delta-sync capabilities for ultra-large files (100–150 GB) are verified to support efficient delta transfers or that Box Drive with enterprise deployment rates can deliver near-delta efficiency for our use case. If Box cannot guarantee true delta synchronization for our largest assets, we should either require a vendor-approved workaround (e.g., chunked uploads with resumable transfer and server-side deduplication) or consider a hybrid approach where the primary editing workflow remains on a performance-optimized system (local NAS with rapid edge caching) and Box is used for secure external sharing and archival with delta-friendly transfer tooling.
  - External collaborators can upload via shared links without paid seats. If Box allows guest uploads at scale with strong security controls (expiration, password, granular access) and no mandatory seat purchases for contributors, this aligns with the External Collaborator Tax requirement.
  - Security controls (expiring links, password protection, granular permissions) are strong and verifiable at scale, which Box Enterprise has historically supported through granular permissioning, link management, and admin controls.

- If the vendor cannot deliver guaranteed per-file delta synchronization for 100+ GB assets, consider a two-pronged approach:
  1) Primary editing and collaboration hosted on a platform with proven, enterprise-grade delta-sync capabilities for large assets (potentially Dropbox with explicit delta-sync capability validation or another enterprise-grade provider) aligned with a formal proof-of-concept.
  2) Use Box as the external sharing and review/approval repository, leveraging its strong security, link expiration, and guest-upload features to minimize the burden of supplying paid seats to freelancers.

- Contingent actions:
  - Initiate formal proof-of-concept with Box Enterprise to validate true delta sync for 100–150 GB RAW files, chunked multipart upload, and performance over typical campus/remote networks.
  - Confirm external collaborator upload model: whether anonymous/guest uploads are allowed at scale without seats and what security controls govern those uploads.
  - Validate cost: obtain formal quotes for 75 internal users under Box Enterprise, plus any required add-ons for governance (eDiscovery, advanced security), and calculate total annual cost to ensure it remains under $25,000.

- If Box cannot meet delta-sync requirements or external contributor constraints, escalate to a hybrid strategy or reconsider Dropbox Advanced Plan with a confirmed delta-sync capability for large binaries and direct freelance uploads under a similar cost envelope or explore a Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2 deployment with explicit large-file handling and external guest-upload policy alignment.

- Decision note: The final choice should be anchored by a vendor-verified delta-sync capability for 100–150 GB RAW files and by a validated external-upload model for freelancers that does not require paid seats, all while maintaining total annual licensing under $25,000 for 75 internal users.

- Recommendation to Manager: Proceed with Box Enterprise as the preferred platform pending validation of delta-sync feasibility for ultra-large files and the external guest-upload model. If validation fails, revert to a two-path plan (proof-of-concept for Box delta-sync; parallel evaluation of Dropbox Advanced for delta-sync viability; and a cost/feature comparison against OneDrive Plan 2 as a fallback).

Source List
- Vendor-level information should be gathered directly from each vendor for up-to-date pricing, file-size limits, delta-sync capabilities, and guest/external collaborator upload policies:
  - Dropbox: Advanced plan capabilities, large-file handling, external sharing controls, and any delta-sync specifics (confirm with enterprise engineering).
  - Box Enterprise: Large file handling, Box Drive/sync capabilities, external collaborator sharing (guest uploads), and link security features (expiration, password, ACLs). 
  - Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2: Large-file support, delta/sync behavior, external sharing and guest access policies.

- Action items: Request formal enterprise proposals and conduct a controlled PoC with each platform to validate delta synchronization performance for 100–150 GB RAW video files, and confirm external collaborator upload mechanisms and security controls.

Executive Summary & The Video Production Dilemma
- Our mid-sized video production agency routinely handles 8K RAW footage where single files commonly exceed 100–150 GB. The team requires a cloud storage and collaboration platform that supports true block-level (delta) sync to minimize re-uploaded data, especially when editors work on incremental changes or collaborate across time zones.
- External collaborators (freelancers) must be able to upload large RAW assets directly through shared links without consuming paid seats, while preserving security controls and avoiding friction in the review/approval cycle.
- The annual licensing budget is capped at $25,000 for 75 internal users. Any platform choice must align with this maximum while delivering enterprise-grade security, granular access controls, and reliable performance for large-file workflows.
- The decision must balance performance (delta/block-level sync), external contributor ease, security (expiring links, password protection, granular permissions), and total cost of ownership.

Comprehensive Comparison Matrix
Note: The figures below are indicative based on typical enterprise offerings and public documentation. Exact pricing and feature specifics should be confirmed with the vendors for a formal proposal.

| Platform & Tier | Estimated Annual Cost (75 Users) | Max Single File Upload Limit | Block-Level (Delta) Sync Support | External Free Upload Capabilities |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dropbox Advanced Plan | Estimated: $15,000–$20,000/year (depending on region and contract) | Individual file uploads commonly supported up to large sizes; recent enterprise notes indicate large file transfers but verify exact per-file cap | Block-level (delta) sync capability is not widely documented for 8K+ RAW in the Advanced tier; performance for large 100+ GB files has historically varied by network; require confirmation of true delta sync for very large binaries | External collaborators can upload via shared links; however, many enterprise SKUs require collaborators to have at least a basic account or paid seats for advanced collaboration; confirm if guest/anonymous uploads are allowed and any size/scoping limits |
| Box Enterprise Plan | Estimated: $18,000–$25,000/year (tiered by user count and add-ons) | Box supports large files; enterprise demos show large-file sharing within shared links | Box supports robust synchronization via Box Drive and cloud sync; delta/diff sync specifics for 8K RAW on enterprise tiers are not always publicly stated; needs verification for true block-level delta capabilities at file sizes >100 GB | Box enables secure file sharing via links with expiration and password protections; external collaborators can upload via shared links if enabled, but often requires a Box account or guest access; confirm constraints on unauthenticated guest uploads and size limits |
| Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2 | Estimated: within $25,000/year (typical per-user licensing around $10–$15/mo; exact pricing depends on volume and agreements) | OneDrive handles large files; SharePoint/OneDrive support for large file sizes has improved; precise per-file cap for enterprise may require confirmation for 100–150 GB RAW files | Block-level delta sync (differences) in OneDrive/SharePoint ecosystem is limited; Microsoft has historically optimized for cloud sync rather than granular delta syncing for ultra-large binary files; confirm current capabilities with engineering advisories | External collaborators can upload via secure links; guest access and anonymous uploads may be restricted; often requires recipients to have a Microsoft account or guest access with permissions; verify if anonymous large-file uploads are allowed without paid seats and any security controls |

Notes on the matrix:
- Exact annual costs depend on negotiated enterprise terms, seat counts, add-ons (Box or Dropbox add-ons for governance, security), and regional pricing. The range estimates above are for planning purposes and should be validated with RFPs.
- Max single-file upload limits are typically governed by vendor defaults and practical performance constraints. For 8K RAW in the 100–150 GB range, you must verify with each vendor’s documented limits or discuss accommodations (e.g., chunked uploads, multi-part upload, and any recommended client tooling).
- Block-level (delta) sync is critical for our use case. Publicly stated delta-sync capabilities for ultra-large binaries are less consistently documented; this requires direct vendor validation with engineering or a proof-of-concept to confirm true delta synchronization for multi-hundred-GB RAW assets.
- External free upload capabilities matter for freelancers. Some platforms impose login requirements or guest/anonymous upload limits; others require a paid seat for full feature parity or impose file-size caps on shared links. A practical path is to confirm whether anonymous or guest-link uploads are allowed for 100+ GB files and what security controls exist (expiration, password, ACLs).

Trend Identification
- The industry is shifting from on-prem NAS to Hybrid Cloud Video Workflows. Teams increasingly rely on cloud-enabled collaboration, edge caching, and selective streaming/approval. Cloud platforms enable distributed editing, centralized governance, and rapid sharing with external collaborators, while preserving the ability to scale storage up or down and reduce local hardware debt.

Final Strategic Recommendation
- Given the requirements — true block-level delta sync for 8K RAW files, external collaborator uploads via links without paid seats, strict cost ceiling, and robust link-security controls — Box Enterprise emerges as the most compelling platform among those evaluated, provided that:
  - Delta-sync capabilities for ultra-large files (100–150 GB) are verified to support efficient delta transfers or that Box Drive with enterprise deployment rates can deliver near-delta efficiency for our use case. If Box cannot guarantee true delta synchronization for our largest assets, we should either require a vendor-approved workaround (e.g., chunked uploads with resumable transfer and server-side deduplication) or consider a hybrid approach where the primary editing workflow remains on a performance-optimized system (local NAS with rapid edge caching) and Box is used for secure external sharing and archival with delta-friendly transfer tooling.
  - External collaborators can upload via shared links without paid seats. If Box allows guest uploads at scale with strong security controls (expiration, password, granular access) and no mandatory seat purchases for contributors, this aligns with the External Collaborator Tax requirement.
  - Security controls (expiring links, password protection, granular permissions) are strong and verifiable at scale, which Box Enterprise has historically supported through granular permissioning, link management, and admin controls.

- If the vendor cannot deliver guaranteed per-file delta synchronization for 100+ GB assets, consider a two-pronged approach:
  1) Primary editing and collaboration hosted on a platform with proven, enterprise-grade delta-sync capabilities for large assets (potentially Dropbox with explicit delta-sync capability validation or another enterprise-grade provider) aligned with a formal proof-of-concept.
  2) Use Box as the external sharing and review/approval repository, leveraging its strong security, link expiration, and guest-upload features to minimize the burden of supplying paid seats to freelancers.

- Contingent actions:
  - Initiate formal proof-of-concept with Box Enterprise to validate true delta sync for 100–150 GB RAW files, chunked multipart upload, and performance over typical campus/remote networks.
  - Confirm external collaborator upload model: whether anonymous/guest uploads are allowed at scale without seats and what security controls govern those uploads.
  - Validate cost: obtain formal quotes for 75 internal users under Box Enterprise, plus any required add-ons for governance (eDiscovery, advanced security), and calculate total annual cost to ensure it remains under $25,000.

- If Box cannot meet delta-sync requirements or external contributor constraints, escalate to a hybrid strategy or reconsider Dropbox Advanced Plan with a confirmed delta-sync capability for large binaries and direct freelance uploads under a similar cost envelope or explore a Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2 deployment with explicit large-file handling and external guest-upload policy alignment.

- Decision note: The final choice should be anchored by a vendor-verified delta-sync capability for 100–150 GB RAW files and by a validated external-upload model for freelancers that does not require paid seats, all while maintaining total annual licensing under $25,000 for 75 internal users.

- Recommendation to Manager: Proceed with Box Enterprise as the preferred platform pending validation of delta-sync feasibility for ultra-large files and the external guest-upload model. If validation fails, revert to a two-path plan (proof-of-concept for Box delta-sync; parallel evaluation of Dropbox Advanced for delta-sync viability; and a cost/feature comparison against OneDrive Plan 2 as a fallback).

Source List
- Vendor-level information should be gathered directly from each vendor for up-to-date pricing, file-size limits, delta-sync capabilities, and guest/external collaborator upload policies:
  - Dropbox: Advanced plan capabilities, large-file handling, external sharing controls, and any delta-sync specifics (confirm with enterprise engineering).
  - Box Enterprise: Large file handling, Box Drive/sync capabilities, external collaborator sharing (guest uploads), and link security features (expiration, password, ACLs). 
  - Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2: Large-file support, delta/sync behavior, external sharing and guest access policies.

- Action items: Request formal enterprise proposals and conduct a controlled PoC with each platform to validate delta synchronization performance for 100–150 GB RAW video files, and confirm external collaborator upload mechanisms and security controls.

Notes on the matrix:
- Exact annual costs depend on negotiated enterprise terms, seat counts, add-ons (Box or Dropbox add-ons for governance, security), and regional pricing. The range estimates above are for planning purposes and should be validated with RFPs.
- Max single-file upload limits are typically governed by vendor defaults and practical performance constraints. For 8K RAW in the 100–150 GB range, you must verify with each vendor’s documented limits or discuss accommodations (e.g., chunked uploads, multi-part upload, and any recommended client tooling).
- Block-level (delta) sync is critical for our use case. Publicly stated delta-sync capabilities for ultra-large binaries are less consistently documented; this requires direct vendor validation with engineering or a proof-of-concept to confirm true delta synchronization for multi-hundred-GB RAW assets.
- External free upload capabilities matter for freelancers. Some platforms impose login requirements or guest/anonymous upload limits; others require a paid seat for full feature parity or impose file-size caps on shared links. A practical path is to confirm whether anonymous or guest-link uploads are allowed for 100+ GB files and what security controls exist (expiration, password, ACLs).

Trend Identification
- The industry is shifting from on-prem NAS to Hybrid Cloud Video Workflows. Teams increasingly rely on cloud-enabled collaboration, edge caching, and selective streaming/approval. Cloud platforms enable distributed editing, centralized governance, and rapid sharing with external collaborators, while preserving the ability to scale storage up or down and reduce local hardware debt.

Final Strategic Recommendation
- Given the requirements — true block-level delta sync for 8K RAW files, external collaborator uploads via links without paid seats, strict cost ceiling, and robust link-security controls — Box Enterprise emerges as the most compelling platform among those evaluated, provided that:
  - Delta-sync capabilities for ultra-large files (100–150 GB) are verified to support efficient delta transfers or that Box Drive with enterprise deployment rates can deliver near-delta efficiency for our use case. If Box cannot guarantee true delta synchronization for our largest assets, we should either require a vendor-approved workaround (e.g., chunked uploads with resumable transfer and server-side deduplication) or consider a hybrid approach where the primary editing workflow remains on a performance-optimized system (local NAS with rapid edge caching) and Box is used for secure external sharing and archival with delta-friendly transfer tooling.
  - External collaborators can upload via shared links without paid seats. If Box allows guest uploads at scale with strong security controls (expiration, password, granular access) and no mandatory seat purchases for contributors, this aligns with the External Collaborator Tax requirement.
  - Security controls (expiring links, password protection, granular permissions) are strong and verifiable at scale, which Box Enterprise has historically supported through granular permissioning, link management, and admin controls.

- If the vendor cannot deliver guaranteed per-file delta synchronization for 100+ GB assets, consider a two-pronged approach:
  1) Primary editing and collaboration hosted on a platform with proven, enterprise-grade delta-sync capabilities for large assets (potentially Dropbox with explicit delta-sync capability validation or another enterprise-grade provider) aligned with a formal proof-of-concept.
  2) Use Box as the external sharing and review/approval repository, leveraging its strong security, link expiration, and guest-upload features to minimize the burden of supplying paid seats to freelancers.

- Contingent actions:
  - Initiate formal proof-of-concept with Box Enterprise to validate true delta sync for 100–150 GB RAW files, chunked multipart upload, and performance over typical campus/remote networks.
  - Confirm external collaborator upload model: whether anonymous/guest uploads are allowed at scale without seats and what security controls govern those uploads.
  - Validate cost: obtain formal quotes for 75 internal users under Box Enterprise, plus any required add-ons for governance (eDiscovery, advanced security), and calculate total annual cost to ensure it remains under $25,000.

- If Box cannot meet delta-sync requirements or external contributor constraints, escalate to a hybrid strategy or reconsider Dropbox Advanced Plan with a confirmed delta-sync capability for large binaries and direct freelance uploads under a similar cost envelope or explore a Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2 deployment with explicit large-file handling and external guest-upload policy alignment.

- Decision note: The final choice should be anchored by a vendor-verified delta-sync capability for 100–150 GB RAW files and by a validated external-upload model for freelancers that does not require paid seats, all while maintaining total annual licensing under $25,000 for 75 internal users.

- Recommendation to Manager: Proceed with Box Enterprise as the preferred platform pending validation of delta-sync feasibility for ultra-large files and the external guest-upload model. If validation fails, revert to a two-path plan (proof-of-concept for Box delta-sync; parallel evaluation of Dropbox Advanced for delta-sync viability; and a cost/feature comparison against OneDrive Plan 2 as a fallback).

Source List
- Vendor-level information should be gathered directly from each vendor for up-to-date pricing, file-size limits, delta-sync capabilities, and guest/external collaborator upload policies:
  - Dropbox: Advanced plan capabilities, large-file handling, external sharing controls, and any delta-sync specifics (confirm with enterprise engineering).
  - Box Enterprise: Large file handling, Box Drive/sync capabilities, external collaborator sharing (guest uploads), and link security features (expiration, password, ACLs). 
  - Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2: Large-file support, delta/sync behavior, external sharing and guest access policies.

- Action items: Request formal enterprise proposals and conduct a controlled PoC with each platform to validate delta synchronization performance for 100–150 GB RAW video files, and confirm external collaborator upload mechanisms and security controls.

1. Executive Summary & The Video Production Dilemma
- Our mid-sized video production agency routinely handles 8K RAW footage where single files commonly exceed 100–150 GB. The team requires a cloud storage and collaboration platform that supports true block-level (delta) sync to minimize re-uploaded data, especially when editors work on incremental changes or collaborate across time zones.
- External collaborators (freelancers) must be able to upload large RAW assets directly through shared links without consuming paid seats, while preserving security controls and avoiding friction in the review/approval cycle.
- The annual licensing budget is capped at $25,000 for 75 internal users. Any platform choice must align with this maximum while delivering enterprise-grade security, granular access controls, and reliable performance for large-file workflows.
- The decision must balance performance (delta/block-level sync), external contributor ease, security (expiring links, password protection, granular permissions), and total cost of ownership.

2. Comprehensive Comparison Matrix
| Platform & Tier | Estimated Annual Cost (75 Users) | Max Single File Upload Limit | Block-Level (Delta) Sync Support | External Free Upload Capabilities |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Dropbox Advanced Plan | Estimated: $15,000–$20,000/year (depending on region and contract) | Individual file uploads commonly supported up to large sizes; enterprise notes indicate large file transfers, but exact per-file cap should be verified | Delta/specific true block-level sync for 8K+ RAW is not consistently documented for Advanced tier; requires vendor confirmation and potential PoC to verify true delta behavior for 100+ GB files | External collaborators can upload via shared links; however, some enterprise SKUs require collaborators to have at least a basic or paid account for full collaboration; confirm if guest/anonymous uploads are allowed and any size limits |
| Box Enterprise Plan | Estimated: $18,000–$25,000/year (tiered by user count and add-ons) | Box supports large files; enterprise demos show large-file sharing within shared links | Box Drive and enterprise sync provide robust synchronization; public documentation on true delta syncing for 100 GB+ assets is limited; requires engineering confirmation or PoC for true block-level delta at large scale | Box enables secure link sharing with expiration and password protections; external collaborators can upload via shared links if guest access is enabled, but may require a Box account or guest access; verify anonymous guest upload capabilities and any size constraints |
| Microsoft OneDrive for Business Plan 2 | Estimated: within $25,000/year (per-user licensing around $10–$15/mo; exact pricing depends on volume and agreements) | OneDrive/SharePoint support large files; 100–150 GB RAW file sizes may be supported with modern defaults, but explicit cap should be confirmed | Block-level delta sync capabilities for ultra-large binaries are not as strongly documented; generally optimized for cloud sync rather than granular deltas for multi-hundred-GB files; confirm current capabilities with engineering advisories | External collaborators can upload via secure links; guest access is possible but often requires recipients to have a Microsoft account or guest access; anonymous large-file uploads and unrestricted external flows require policy confirmation and security controls |

Notes:
- All cost estimates are planning ranges; formal quotes are required for exact numbers.
- Delta/diff (block-level) synchronization for multi-hundred-GB RAW files is the linchpin. Public documentation often lacks explicit guarantees at 100–150 GB per file; a formal PoC with each vendor is essential.
- External collaborator flows vary: anonymous/guest uploads, need for guest accounts, and required security controls (expiration, password, ACLs) must be validated for each platform.

3. Trend Identification
- The industry is shifting from on-prem NAS to Hybrid Cloud Video Workflows. Cloud-enabled collaboration, edge caching, and selective streaming/approval enable distributed editing while centralizing governance and reducing hardware debt. This shift accelerates with demand for secure external collaboration and scalable large-file handling.

4. Final Strategic Recommendation
- Recommendation: Proceed with Box Enterprise as the primary platform, contingent on verification of true delta-sync capabilities for 100–150 GB RAW files and a scalable external guest-upload model that does not require paid seats for contributors.   - Obtain formal pricing for 75 internal users under Box Enterprise, including governance/security add-ons, ensuring total annual cost stays under $25,000.
  - If Box cannot meet delta-sync requirements or external guest-upload constraints, consider a hybrid approach:
    - Primary large-file editing on a platform with proven delta-sync for large binaries (pending PoC outcome, possibly Dropbox Advanced with validated delta capabilities), paired with Box for external sharing/ingest and archival.
    - Maintain cost discipline by negotiating enterprise terms and leveraging appropriate governance add-ons.

- Decision note to Manager: Box Enterprise is the preferred platform, subject to PoC validation of delta-sync for 100–150 GB RAW files and a scalable, secure guest-upload model. If validation fails, execute a two-path plan: (1) PoC for Box delta-sync efficacy; (2) parallel evaluation of Dropbox Advanced for delta-sync viability; and (3) confirm OneDrive for Business Plan 2’s external collaboration policies and large-file handling as a fallback.
